intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The uAgents framework runtime gives every deployed agent a cryptographic identity and Fetch.ai wallet, registers its address and endpoints on the Almanac, and exchanges typed, signed messages with other agents over an authenticated HTTP channel. Its declared security duty is a protected key identity, tamper-evident and replay-resistant inter-agent messaging, schema-validated inputs, and bounded, observable operation. Private keys, wallet keys, and seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext; administrative and message-history interfaces MUST NOT be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers, and binding to a public interface or exposing the inspector requires operator approval. The remit evaluates the runtime's default posture handed to every agent, not any single deployed agent.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python framework (packages uagents and uagents-core, v0.25.3) built on uvicorn/ASGI, pydantic, ecdsa/bech32 for SECP256k1 identity, and cosmpy for the Fetch.ai wallet/ledger. Inbound envelopes arrive at /submit on an ASGIServer (src/uagents/asgi.py) whose host is the hardcoded module constant HOST = "0.0.0.0"; the same server registers the inspector REST endpoints /agent_info, /messages, /connect, and /disconnect when enable_agent_inspector (default True) is set in agent.py. Envelope signing/verification lives in uagents-core/uagents_core/envelope.py; the name-based key path in src/uagents/storage/__init__.py persists identity and wallet keys to a cleartext private_keys.json. The server sets forwarded_allow_ips="*" and its reserved-endpoint guard trusts a client source of 127.0.0.1, while the inbound dispatch path enforces neither the envelope expires nor nonce fields.

behavior_summary: The framework ships real cryptographic primitives — ECDSA envelope signing and verification, unit-tested — but the default runtime posture handed to every agent contradicts several of the remit's hardest prohibitions at once. The dominant pattern is an unauthenticated-remote-admin chain: the server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default, its inspector endpoints are enabled by default, and the only guard on the administrative endpoints is a source-IP check for 127.0.0.1 that forwarded_allow_ips="*" makes trivially spoofable with an X-Forwarded-For header — exactly the remit's "spoofed loopback source address" bad-behavior example. Compounding this, identity and wallet private keys are written to disk in cleartext, and inbound signed envelopes are dispatched with no replay or expiry enforcement, so a captured envelope re-runs its handler. Signature verification protects message integrity, but freshness, key-at-rest protection, and administrative access control are all absent by default.

rules.rule_text: Executing operating-system shell commands or arbitrary code on behalf of a remote message sender.
Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.
Sensitive configuration secrets MUST NOT be embedded as literals in framework or agent source.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.
Dispatching an inbound message from a non-user agent sender whose envelope signature has not been verified.
Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded; registration and delivery retries must be finite.
Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Alert Operator (Do Not Halt)
rules.rule_text: The agent server is bound to a public interface.

rules.section: Known Good Baseline — Normal Restart Cadence
rules.rule_text: Restarts must not change a seeded agent's identity or wallet address.
Receipt of a message with an unrecognized schema digest.

findings.summary: The name-based key path writes agent identity and wallet private keys to a cleartext private_keys.json in the working directory.
findings.description: When an agent is constructed with a name but no seed, get_or_create_private_keys persists both the identity private key and the wallet private key to private_keys.json as plaintext JSON in the current working directory, with no encryption, keychain, or secret-manager path. This directly violates the remit's prohibition on writing private keys or seeds to disk in plaintext and exposes on-chain wallet control to anyone who reads the file.
findings.policy_rule_text: Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form. / Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.

evidence.snippet: save_private_keys writes {"identity_key": ..., "wallet_key": ...} to private_keys.json via json.dump in cleartext (lines 113-127)
_initialize_wallet_and_identity calls get_or_create_private_keys(name) on the no-seed path, triggering plaintext persistence of both keys

recommended_actions: Encrypt keys at rest — derive a data-encryption key from an operator-supplied passphrase or delegate to an OS keychain / secret manager, and never write the raw identity or wallet private key to private_keys.json.
At minimum, restrict the file to 0600 and document that seed-from-environment (never name-based persistence) is the supported production path.

findings.summary: Default public bind plus a spoofable loopback guard let unauthenticated remote callers reach the inspector and administrative endpoints.
findings.description: The server binds to the hardcoded 0.0.0.0, the agent inspector is enabled by default, and the only protection on the reserved admin endpoints is a check that scope["client"] contains 127.0.0.1 — but forwarded_allow_ips="*" makes uvicorn rewrite that client from an attacker-supplied X-Forwarded-For header, so a remote caller sending X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1 bypasses the guard to read message history via /messages and re-register or unregister the agent via /connect and /disconnect, while /agent_info is exempt from the guard entirely. This is the remit's "spoofed loopback source address" bad-behavior example. Contributing links: the 0.0.0.0 bind is broken out as PRAX-2026-07-17-004 (public exposure of /submit is a finding on its own); the forwarded_allow_ips spoof, the default-on inspector, and the /agent_info guard exemption are folded here because each is exploitable only because the server is remotely reachable.
findings.policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers. / Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers / Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.

evidence.snippet: reserved-endpoint guard returns 403 only when "127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"] (lines 304-311); combined with forwarded_allow_ips="*" at line 184, scope["client"] is taken from a spoofable X-Forwarded-For header, and /agent_info is excluded from the guarded set
enable_agent_inspector defaults to True, registering /agent_info, /messages, /connect, /disconnect REST handlers (lines 474-531) reachable on the 0.0.0.0-bound server

recommended_actions: Do not trust a proxy-supplied client address for the localhost check — drop forwarded_allow_ips="*" (or restrict it to a known reverse-proxy IP) and gate the admin endpoints on an authenticated operator credential rather than source IP.
Default enable_agent_inspector to False and require explicit operator opt-in, and remove /agent_info from the unguarded set so no reserved endpoint is remotely reachable without approval.

findings.summary: Inbound signed envelopes are dispatched with no replay or expiry enforcement, so a captured envelope re-runs its handler.
findings.description: The envelope carries optional expires and nonce fields, but the inbound path verifies only the signature and then dispatches unconditionally — expires is read solely to size the sync-response timeout, never to reject a stale envelope, and nonce and session are never checked for reuse. A previously delivered, validly-signed envelope can therefore be replayed and its handler executed again, exactly the "Never Allowed" replay/expired behavior the remit forbids. The framework already implements nonce+validity attestation for external storage, so the freshness primitive exists but is not applied to inter-agent messages.
findings.policy_rule_text: Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh. / Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

evidence.snippet: after env.verify() the handler dispatches via dispatch_msg (lines 363-385) with no expiry or nonce/session replay check; expires is only consulted at line 390 to compute the sync-response timeout
expires and nonce are optional (default None) and only folded into _digest when present (lines 39-40, 105-110); verify() checks the signature alone

recommended_actions: Reject envelopes whose expires timestamp is in the past before dispatch, and require expires on the security-relevant path so it cannot be omitted.
Track recently-seen (sender, session, nonce) tuples within the validity window and drop duplicates so a replayed envelope is not re-dispatched.

findings.summary: The server host is the hardcoded constant 0.0.0.0, so every agent binds to a public interface with no approval gate and no way to choose loopback.
findings.description: HOST is a module-level constant set to 0.0.0.0 in asgi.py and is not exposed as an Agent or serve() parameter, so the runtime always binds to all interfaces with no operator opt-in and no alert. The remit requires human approval before binding to a non-loopback interface and requires an operator alert when the server is public; neither exists. This is the root exposure that makes the admin-access chain (PRAX-2026-07-17-002) remotely reachable, and independently exposes the /submit message-intake surface to the public internet.
findings.policy_rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface. / The agent server is bound to a public interface.

evidence.snippet: HOST = "0.0.0.0" module constant, passed to uvicorn.Config(host=HOST, ...) in serve() (lines 177-198) with no loopback default or configuration parameter

recommended_actions: Default the bind host to 127.0.0.1 and make it a configurable Agent parameter, requiring an explicit operator choice to bind a public interface.
Emit a startup warning/alert whenever the server binds to a non-loopback address, as the remit's escalation rule requires.

findings.summary: On the user/unsigned path the sender address is accepted as asserted, with identity determined only by a "user" string prefix and no signature check.
findings.description: The server skips signature verification whenever is_user_address(env.sender) is true, and is_user_address only tests that the address string starts with "user", so a remote caller can present any user-prefixed sender and have it passed to unsigned handlers as the message origin. The framework does correctly reject user senders for signed handlers, so this is a partial control, but any handler using the unsigned/allow_unverified path receives a spoofable sender identity that must not be trusted for a security decision, as the remit warns.
findings.policy_rule_text: Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity. / Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.

evidence.snippet: "if not is_user_address(env.sender): env.verify()" — verification is skipped entirely for user-prefixed senders before dispatch_msg
is_user_address returns address[0:4] == "user" — a string-prefix check with no cryptographic proof of the claimed user identity

recommended_actions: Document prominently that env.sender is unauthenticated on the user/unsigned path and must never gate a security decision, and surface it in the handler context as untrusted.
Where user senders must be trusted, require a signed attestation (the framework already has compute_attestation) rather than a bare user-prefixed address.

findings.summary: The inbound /submit path applies no rate limiting, connection cap, or request-size limit, leaving the public server open to flooding and denial-of-wallet.
findings.description: The ASGI handler reads the entire request body into memory and dispatches with no per-sender throttle, no concurrency cap, and no maximum payload size, and no such control exists in config.py. On the default 0.0.0.0 bind this is a public endpoint, so an attacker can flood /submit to exhaust CPU/memory or, for agents that trigger paid ledger or LLM work per message, drive denial-of-wallet. Signature verification does not bound this, since verification itself is the work being amplified.

evidence.snippet: _read_asgi_body reads the full body then dispatches (lines 281-385) with no rate limit, connection cap, or size ceiling; config.py defines no rate-limit constant

recommended_actions: Add a configurable per-sender/per-IP rate limit and a maximum request-body size to the /submit intake, rejecting oversized or excessive requests before verification and dispatch.
Expose a concurrency cap so a burst of inbound envelopes cannot exhaust the event loop.

findings.summary: The cosmpy wallet/ledger dependency is floor-pinned with no upper bound, so consumers can silently pull an untested or malicious future major version.
findings.description: uagents declares cosmpy (>=0.12.1) with no upper ceiling while most other dependencies carry version caps and committed uv.lock/poetry.lock files pin the build environment. Because cosmpy is the component that handles wallet keys, signing, and ledger transactions — the most trust-sensitive dependency in the stack — an unbounded range exposes downstream agents to a breaking or compromised future major version through ordinary dependency resolution, with no SBOM to track exposure.

evidence.snippet: dependencies list pins cosmpy (>=0.12.1) with no upper bound, unlike pydantic/uvicorn/aiohttp which carry <-caps; no SBOM/ML-BOM is published for consumers

recommended_actions: Add an upper version bound on cosmpy (e.g. <1.0 or the next known-good major) to prevent silent adoption of an untested major, and re-evaluate on each cosmpy release.
Publish an SBOM for the framework so downstream operators can assess exposure when a dependency CVE lands.

findings.summary: A signature-verification failure produces only a free-form warning log, with no operator alert or structured security-event record.
findings.description: When env.verify() fails the server logs a warning and returns a 400, but there is no operator alert, no structured security-event emission, and no halt — the remit's escalation rule calls for halting and alerting the operator on a signature-verification failure. Logs are free-form text through Python logging with no schema, so repeated verification failures (a signal of active spoofing or tampering) cannot drive automated detection. This is an observability/alerting gap, not an exploitable control on its own.
findings.policy_rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

evidence.snippet: failed verification logs self._logger.warning(f"Failed to verify envelope: {err}") and returns 400 (lines 366-371); no alert, no structured event, no halt

recommended_actions: Emit a structured, machine-parseable security event on signature-verification failure (sender, session, source) so repeated failures can be alerted on.
Provide an operator-configurable alert hook for verification failures rather than relying on a warning buried in stdout logs.

positives.title: ECDSA envelope signing and verification, unit-tested
positives.description: Inter-agent envelopes are signed with the agent's SECP256k1 identity and verified against the sender address before dispatch, and the path is covered by unit tests — a genuine, operative message-integrity control.

positives.title: Deterministic seed-derived identity keeps a restarted agent stable
positives.description: Identity and wallet keys are derived deterministically from an operator seed, so a seeded agent keeps the same address and wallet across restarts as the remit requires.

positives.title: Signed-message handlers reject unverified user senders
positives.description: When a handler is registered for signed messages, a message from an unverified user-prefixed sender is rejected with an error rather than dispatched, enforcing the signed/unsigned boundary.

positives.title: Inbound payloads are schema-validated before dispatch
positives.description: Every inbound envelope is parsed and validated against its declared pydantic model before the payload reaches a handler, constraining untrusted input at the intake boundary.

rows.purpose: uagents-core runtime/application events written to cwd by default
agent server/runtime lifecycle, warnings including signature-verification failures

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The runtime has a genuine, tested authentication primitive — ECDSA envelope signing and verification with deterministic seed-derived identity — and validates every inbound payload against its pydantic schema, which lifts Balance and keeps Zero Trust off the floor. But the Zero Trust surface that matters most is undermined by defaults: plaintext key storage, a public bind with no approval gate, a spoofable loopback guard on the inspector, and no replay or expiry enforcement on signed envelopes. Supply-chain provenance is reasonable (committed lockfiles, version caps) but a crypto/wallet dependency is left unbounded; monitoring is present-but-unstructured with no security-event alerting; and there is no evidence of adversarial testing beyond functional unit tests.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The runtime scopes its own surface to a fixed set of reserved endpoints and dispatches only registered message schemas by digest, but the administrative/inspector surface is enabled by default and over-exposed rather than opt-in, widening the reachable surface beyond the remit's local-operator-only intent.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Every inbound envelope payload is validated against a declared pydantic message schema before dispatch (asgi.py model_validate, agent.py parse_raw), a consistently-applied data-intake control; the known gap is that the sender identity on the user/unsigned path is accepted as asserted.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: ECDSA envelope signature verification is implemented and tested for agent senders, but plaintext key storage, a hardcoded public bind with no approval gate, a spoofable loopback guard on inspector endpoints, and complete absence of replay/expiry enforcement leave the trust boundary porous by default.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Provenance is known and uv.lock/poetry.lock are committed with upper-bounded ranges on most dependencies, but the cosmpy crypto/wallet dependency is floor-pinned with no upper bound and there is no SBOM/ML-BOM for the framework consumers inherit.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The test suite covers signing/verification and protocol behavior functionally (tests/test_msg_verify.py and peers) but there is no fuzzing, injection, replay, or adversarial-testing artifact and no evidence that security testing drove architectural change.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The framework logs through Python logging to stdout and uagents-core adds a default uagents_core.log file handler, but logs are free-form text with no structured security-event schema and signature-verification failures produce only a warning with no operator alert.
